data.title: Kimbell Royalty Partners: A High-Margin And Sustainable Business Model Justify An Upside
data.description:
Three months after my second analysis, Kimbell Royalty Partners LP (NYSE:KRP) still faces unfavorable market dynamics. Oil and gas prices remain weak as geopolitical tensions and macroeconomic volatility persist. Yet, its high-margin, asset-light business model and well-positioned Balance Sheet continue to insulate it from headwinds. Valuation adheres to these aspects as the stock continues to trade at a reasonable level. Technicals are still weak today, but new buying chances are appearing again.

Challenges

Kimbell Royalty Partners LP only owns mineral and royalty interests. However, it is important to note that oil and gas price volatility can still affect its performance. Why? Kimbell earns royalty interests or a portion of the revenues generated by oil companies using KRP’s land/ mineral rights. If the selling prices of oil and gas decrease, KRP’s royalty interest will also decrease.

This is what we saw in its performance during Q2 2025. Its operating revenue amounted to $86.6M, up by 13.1% YoY from $76.6M. Yet, revenues from oil, gas, and NGLs weakened by -2.9% to $74.7M from $77.0M YoY despite the YoY increase in its run-rate daily oil production.

If we can remember, oil prices dropped to their 12-month low at sub-$60 levels. For instance, WTI Crude hit $57-58 per barrel, while Brent landed on $60-62 per barrel. They exceeded $70 in June amid the Israel-Iran tension but immediately went back to sub-$70 after the ceasefire news. For the remainder of the year, oil prices may remain at their current range or at least lower than in 2024. This is despite the current Israel-Qatar tension. One important aspect to consider is the larger-than-expected OPEC oil hike of 548,000 barrels per day recently. The organization will reduce it to 137,000 barrels per day next month. But this still exceeds the supply last year, which may offset the impact of the Middle East Tension. With that, the weighted average fixed price of oil and natural gas for Q3 and Q4 2025 is lower than in the same period in 2024.

Mitigants/Drivers: My Bullish View

Despite the challenges it faces, KRP has some mitigants and even drivers to protect its business from these headwinds. Its high-margin business model itself is an insulation against macroeconomic volatility. It owns mineral interests but does not drill or produce oil, natural gas, and NGLs. So, it has very low cost sensitivity. It does not have to worry about inventory management and fuel expenses. It does not have to worry about cost pressures due to rising prices. Since all it does is acquire mineral interests and have them used by actual oil drillers and producers who will pay them a portion of their revenues or royalty. Although royalties typically decrease with the current oil price, KRP is sure to receive. It has constant revenue streams to cover its operations and cash distributions. On top of that, it receives lease bonuses and gains on its hedging strategies through derivatives. Indeed, its continued mineral rights acquisitions lead to more secure revenues.

As we can see, it has consistent revenue streams, which are enough to sustain its expanding operations and cash distributions. In Q2 2025, the company maintained positive profits. Its operating margin reached 43.7%. This is much higher than 31.2% YoY and 40.2% in my previous coverage.

With respect to its sustainability, KRP remains promising with its robust Balance Sheet. Its cash level has remained stable and increasing in the past 11 quarters. This should not be surprising since KRP is not capital-intensive. Its operating cash flow is enough to cover its capex. Unless it acquires new mineral rights, its earnings alone can cover its business capacity and cash distributions. And you can see that its capex in some quarters is zero.

To sustain its expansion or acquisition, KRP complements it with prudent debt management. It is good to know that the whole amount will not mature within this year. To be more specific, its maturity profile is up to June 2027. This is also consistent with its Net Debt/EBITDA of 1.7x, which means that KRP can pay all its debt at once in less than two years even if it doesn’t have to. Hence, its capital buffer remains enough for its financial obligations and further expansion.

On a broader view, I believe KRP will see new growth prospects. Its mineral rights in nearly 30 states promise longevity in line with its well-positioned Balance Sheet. A large portion of it is in Texas and New Mexico, which are among the top five natural gas producers. Also, these two states are the two major oil producers in the US. With that, KRP is sure that its mineral rights and royalties will last long.

More interestingly, it may see new opportunities in natural gas as it remains the primary electricity source. Its demand will be bolstered by the sustained data center expansion. In fact, data centers are expected to increase their capacity by 15% every year. In the coming years, electricity and natural gas demand will rise.

Valuation

Kimbell Royalty slightly decreased from my previous coverage. In my view, this was logical considering the high correlation between cash per share and stock price explained before. From the CPS of $0.42 in 2024, it went down to $0.38 in my previous coverage, and the stock price weakened from $15.91 to $14.29. In Q2 2025, cash per share decreased to $0.37, and so the stock price followed.

Despite this, the stock still appears to be decreasing much lower and faster than it should, leading to undervaluation. Using P/CPS, KRP trades at 35.73x its CPS versus the five-year average of 46.46x. This is also the second-lowest valuation from 2020 to the present time. If we use this to derive its fair value, the TP will be $17.19. Even if we add a 10% risk consideration, the TP will still be higher at $15.47.

To have a more forward-looking analysis, I also made a DCF Model. I set its perpetual growth rate at 2.0% to be conservative and consistent with the US inflation. Meanwhile, I estimated WACC using the Capital Asset Pricing Model. I set its risk premium at 5.0%, in line with the standard rate. I computed its Beta on MS Excel by applying the daily price change of the stock versus the S&P 500. At 0.57, this is almost the same as the one reported on Yahoo Finance at 0.56, so I decided to use my estimated value.

Lastly, I estimated its FCFF based on my revenue estimation. To be more realistic, I based it on its trend in the past four quarters instead of using the CAGR. I got $304M. For COGS and operating expenses, I set them as a percentage of revenue at 5.8% and 62%, respectively. I did the same to the operating assets and liabilities, except for accounts receivable using DSO and accounts payable using DPO. And for its capex and depreciation, I just used the straight line method for simplicity. With all this information, the TP will be $16.58, which is close to the P/CPS TP. If I add a 10% risk consideration, the TP will only be $14.92 but still higher than the current price. Hence, KRP is reasonably cheap.

Technicals

KRP’s momentum weakens again after its rally from April to August. The price has already crossed below the 50-day and 200-day SMA, which confirms its downtrend. Meanwhile, the 50-day SMA has already flattened slightly below the 200-day SMA. This shows that recent market sentiments have neutralized as investors have become cautious. Investors must beware of a bull trap and a possible reversal.

MACD and RSI reflect these trends as they remain consistent with the price trend. The MACD line and histogram remain below the signal line, showing that selling volume is still higher than buying volume. On a lighter note, we can see some divergences in the histogram, which I think is reasonable. Although it stays red, it starts to get close to zero or neutral. This means that selling volume has weakened recently. We can also see its net buying volume in the past two days. This can tell us that some investors saw the new low as an ideal support level to enter and/or re-enter. RSI also explains the recent dip and rebound attempts due to overbuying last July 29 and overselling last August 19. With that, there is still wide room for buying.

Conclusion

Kimbell Royalty Partners, LP is not fully insulated from oil and gas price volatility. Yet, its business model ensures very low cost sensitivity and capital intensity to stay highly profitable and liquid. That is why I believe it’s reasonably cheap, as valuation shows decent upside. Technicals are still bearish but present early buying opportunities. I’m reiterating my buy rating on this stock.

data.title: Markets Weekly Outlook - PMI And PCE In The Spotlight As U.S. Dollar Remains Sensitive To U.S. Labor Data
data.description:
By Zain Vawda

Week in review - Fed Delivers Cut but Keeps Markets in Check

A busy week that was still dominated by the highly anticipated Federal Reserve meeting. I have to say, hats off to Fed Chair Powell, who kept markets in check, whether you think he is right or wrong in his decision. Believe me, there is support in both camps.

Fed Chair Powell in particular has been under pressure from the political sphere, while labor data and mixed economic signals put the Fed Chair in the firing line. The Fed board itself faced a key decision as markets have turned extremely dovish in expectations ahead of the meeting.

The message from the Fed balanced market expectations while not giving too much away and pushing back, to some degree at least, the questions of Fed independence.

So how did the markets perform?

The S&P 500 and the Nasdaq stock indexes are on track to have their third consecutive week of gains. This positive trend was fueled by the Federal Reserve's first interest rate cut of 2025 and hints that more relaxed monetary policies could be on the way. A renewed sense of optimism around stocks related to artificial intelligence (AI) also contributed to the market's rise.

However, the US stock market was a bit unsteady earlier in the day. Investors were still trying to understand the Fed's future plans and were paying close attention to comments made by Stephen Miran, the newest Fed governor and a White House economic adviser, who spoke on CNBC on Friday morning.

Also on Friday, US President Donald Trump and Chinese President Xi Jinping spoke on the phone, and afterward, Trump announced that they had made progress on a deal for TikTok. He also said that the two leaders had agreed to a meeting in person next month in South Korea.

So far in September, the three main US stock indexes—the Dow Jones Industrial Average, the S&P 500, and the Nasdaq Composite—are all performing well. This is unusual because September has historically been a difficult month for the US stock market. Data shows that since the year 2000, the S&P 500 has, on average, lost 1.4% of its value during this month.

How has the US Dollar Reacted?

The US Dollar has been resilient since the decision and not surprising considering that what the Fed delivered was more hawkish than expected.

For the Fed decision impact, read Caution Over Speed: How the Fed Framed Its First Cut

However, the Fed met expectations by announcing its first rate cut of the year and indicating there would be two more cuts. This caused the dollar to immediately drop by about 0.5% against other currencies.

But within half an hour, the dollar had regained all of its lost value as US government bond yields started to rise again. This quick reversal was likely due to how traders were positioned in the market, rather than a change in how they viewed the Fed's announcement. It was a "trader's market"—meaning it was influenced more by short-term trading behaviors than by long-term economic signals.

The US Dollar index (DXY) is ending the week with 3 successive days in the green.

US Dollar Index Daily Chart, September 19, 2025

Despite this rebound, the long-term outlook for the dollar doesn't seem very positive. The Fed has officially stated that the risk to its two main goals—stable prices and maximum employment—is now more focused on a weaker job market. With the expectation of two more rate cuts this year, bringing the policy rate down to 3.00-3.25%, the dollar could weaken. When the immediate market excitement dies down, the dollar is likely to fall back toward its lowest levels of the year and will become very sensitive to upcoming US job market data.

The Week Ahead - Global PMIs and US PCE

Next week is a busy one, with Flash PMI survey data providing a key focus for the markets in the coming week, though Friday's release of the US core PCE price index will also be eagerly awaited.

Other releases of note include revised US GDP numbers, consumer confidence data for the US and Europe, plus US home sales, durable goods orders and inventories.

Asia Pacific Markets - Tokyo CPI

High impact data will be a bit sparse from Asia next week, with the biggest data release being from Japan.

Tokyo CPI data will be released after the BoJ held rates steady but with a hawkish shift on Friday. Two officials on the central bank's board unexpectedly voted against the majority, showing a more "hawkish" view—meaning they are more concerned about inflation and are in favor of raising interest rates.

The market was also caught off guard by the central bank's announcement that it would begin selling its holdings of exchange-traded funds (ETFs) and Japanese real estate investment trusts (J-REITs). This move is a strong sign that the Bank of Japan (BoJ) is serious about gradually returning its monetary policy to normal.

Based on these signals, I believe that an interest rate hike is a probability in October.

Global PMI and US PCE Data in Focus

Over the next week, several officials from the Federal Reserve (the Fed) will be speaking publicly. This is an important opportunity to hear their individual views on the economy after the Fed recently decided to resume cutting interest rates. They'll likely provide more details on how they see the risks to the economy, especially after signaling that their main forecast is for two more rate cuts this year and one in the next.

The most important data release will be the core personal consumer expenditure (PCE) deflator on Friday. This is the inflation measure the Fed prefers to use.

While the core consumer price index (CPI) rose a bit more than expected last month, the core PCE is likely to show a more modest increase. This is because it gives less weight to housing costs and includes different data like airline fares and healthcare costs. If the core PCE comes in as expected, it would give the Fed a clear signal to move forward with more rate cuts in October and December.

Additionally, new housing market data will be released. With more homes available for sale but still weak demand from buyers, there are growing concerns that home prices could start to fall.

Looking at the Euro Area and based on recent data, business activity in August, measured by PMIs (Purchasing Managers' Indexes), was very positive, primarily because of a significant increase in manufacturing.

However, a separate survey from the European Commission suggests that this boost might be a one-time event, as future expectations for the manufacturing sector weren't particularly strong.

For September, this creates a question for economists: Will the positive mood from the summer continue, or was August's good performance just a brief exception? We think the latter is very possible, especially given that the economy is currently growing at a slow pace.

Chart of the Week - Gold (XAU/USD)

This week's Chart of the Week is Gold.

From a technical standpoint, Gold pulled back after the FOMC meeting and retested the bull flag pattern breakout from Monday.

A bullish move since leaves gold on course for another week of gains above 1%. Gold is trading just shy of the $3700/oz handle.

A weekly close above this level seems unlikely this late in the day, which leaves Gold in a precarious position heading into the new week.

Looking at the four-hour timeframe, Gold has recorded a change in structure but could be in for a short-term pullback before continuing higher.

Gold has seen its price target updated by many institutions, as a combination of potential US Fed rate cuts, along with continued central bank buying and ETF inflows, are likely to keep Gold supported.

That, of course, does not rule out small price retracements in the interim, and that could come into play at some stage next week if profit-taking does occur.

If the US Dollar index retreats next week, that could be another factor which could influence the trajectory of Gold prices, so keep an eye on that.

Immediate support rests at 3666 before the 3656 and 3627 handles come into focus.

Looking at the upside, immediate resistance rests at 3700 before all-time highs at 3707 come into focus.

Gold Four-Hour Chart - September 19, 2025

Trade Safe.

data.title: Equities Supported By Improvement In Business Cycle Indicators
data.description:
By Magdalena Ocampo, Market Strategist

The recent run in equities appears to be at odds with weakening labor market signals, which has led to questions about the sustainability of the rally and the broader health of the U.S. economy. However, resilience in consumer spending and capital expenditures continues to underpin this year’s earnings momentum.

Going forward, stimulative monetary and fiscal policies, along with improvements in lagging sectors such as manufacturing and housing, could further support the economy and extend the equity market rally.

S&P 500 Index vs. Labor Market and Business Cycle Surprise Indices

Percent difference between forecasts and actual data releases, YTD

Source: Bloomberg, Principal Asset Management. Data as of September 17, 2025.

An acknowledgement of the recent weakness in employment trends backed the Fed’s decision to cut policy rates at its September meeting. While the Fed’s projections hint at a labor market in “curious balance,” concerns about the hiring environment still cast uncertainty over the health of the U.S. economy.

Despite labor demand eroding for much of the year, and following April’s Liberation Day selloff, the S&P 500 has reached record highs. The divergence between market strength and labor weakness has raised questions about the true underpinnings and sustainability of the market rally.

Behind challenged labor market headlines, underlying consumer and capex resilience remains a backbone of this year’s earnings strength. Additionally, business cycle leading indicators - ISM Services and regional manufacturing Fed surveys - have surprised to the upside in recent months. The broader picture of economic robustness, further supported by tight credit spreads, continues to underpin the strength in the equity market.

Looking ahead, the combination of monetary easing alongside fiscal and regulatory stimulus could bolster an economy that has proven resilient to this year’s headwinds.

A policy-driven recovery may help revive the more sluggish segments of the economy, including housing, manufacturing, and employment, suggesting that equities, beyond the large tech leaders, could be poised for further modest gains.

data.title: PFL: Inconsistent Earnings Warrants Caution
data.description:
Overview

With the first interest rate cut of 2025 taking place, it'll be interesting to see how income funds react. I believe that funds that utilize a portfolio of debt investments will experience some upside momentum into the renewed optimism around the debt markets. PIMCO Income Strategy Fund (NYSE:PFL) utilizes a portfolio of diverse debt investments and may be able to improve its internal performance in a lower interest rate environment. When I previously covered PFL, I issued a hold rating. However, PFL has released an updated annual report since the time of my last coverage, so I wanted to revisit this fund to provide some updated insights into its performance and outlook for 2025.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that PFL's share price has remained fairly steady. While the fund did decline earlier in the year, it was able to ride the positive momentum of the market to recover. When including all distributions that were paid out, the total return jumps up to 12.8% over the same time frame. PFL now offers investors a starting dividend yield of about 11.5% and monthly payouts, which makes it an attractive choice for income investors.

According to the latest annual report, PFL's ability to cover the distribution with earnings has been inconsistent. This inconsistency makes me cautious about the overall sustainability of distributions going forward. Additionally, the income received from PFL is not the most tax-efficient, which limits the appeal for investors that utilize these sort of high-yield funds within a regular taxable brokerage account. So let's start by taking a look at the fund's valuation and most updated report.

Updated Valuation

At the time of my last coverage, PFL traded at a small premium to NAV of 3.08%. Following the recent market rally, PFL now trades at a higher premium to NAV of approximately 6.38%. For reference, PFL has traded at an average premium to NAV of 6.08% over the last five year period. In order to better conceptualize the current premium in relation to PFL's historical range, I added the red line to the graph below. Even though the premium is slightly higher, PFL still trades within a reasonable range at this time.

To get clarity on the fund's internal performance, I reviewed the latest annual report for 2025. Net investment income amounted to $0.82 per share, while net realized gains landed at $0.28 per share. Looking back on prior years, PFL's net investment income levels have generally remained within a consistent range, which indicates that the fund's strategy of generating income from its holdings is efficient. The only issue is that net investment income isn't high enough to support distributions on its own, which leaves PFL reliant on positive net realized gains to make up the difference.

The problem is that net realized gains are very inconsistent and dependent on positive market conditions so that PFL can sell securities at a higher valuation than they were purchased for. Therefore, PFL offers no downside protection against market declines, which is why we can see that the fund experienced a large net realized loss of $2.11 per share during 2022's market decline. This reliance on positive net realized gains has stunted the fund's ability to provide an attractive level of NAV growth over the last few years. For reference, here are the year-end NAV amounts:

  • 2019: $11.00 per share
  • 2020: $9.46 per share
  • 2021: $10.66 per share
  • 2022: $8.39 per share
  • 2023: $7.77 per share
  • 2024: $7.84 per share
  • 2025: $7.99 per share

The Fed has recently implemented a 25 basis point interest rate cut, which should serve as a positive catalyst for the fund. A lower interest rate may increase the overall sentiment of debt investments, since the cost of borrowing decreases. A lower interest rate may cause an influx of new borrowers in the market, which funds like PFL can use as an opportunity to grow their base assets. Additionally, a lower interest rate may help stimulate the growth of its underlying holdings and improve the level of net realized gains. However, it is still too early to definitively say that this smaller interest rate cut will have a significant impact on PFL's ability to generate better earnings.

Fund Strategy & Risk Profile

According to the latest fact sheet, PFL has total managed assets of $415.8M that are spread across a diverse range of debt investments. The fund utilizes a mix of floating-rate and fixed-rate securities, while focusing primarily on below investment credit. Funds generally utilize these lower-rated securities as a way to generate higher yields in exchange for the risk they may carry. To mitigate these risks, PFL spreads its assets across a wide range of different structures, with a majority allocation on high-yield credit. The breakdown by security type is as follows:

  • High Yield Credit:31.7%
  • US Government Related: 14.3%
  • Non-US Developed 12.7%
  • Non-Agency Mortgage: 11.9%
  • Investment Grade Credit: 9.2%
  • Emerging Markets: 9.0%
  • Other: 8.4%
  • CMBS: 4.4%
  • Agency MBS: 0.9%

Expanding on this diversity, PFL also spreads its assets across a wide range of industries. For instance, PFL is most heavily allocated to Technology holdings at 8.1% of assets. This is followed by exposure to Healthcare and Wirelines investments, which both account for 6.9% of assets, respectively. Despite the diverse range of assets, I believe that investors should limit their expectations for the fund to provide a meaningful level of capital appreciation over time, since PFL's main objective is income generation.

To demonstrate this, I pulled in a performance chart over the last decade. As we can see below, PFL's share price has declined by about 15.44% over the last decade. When including all distributions that were paid out over the last ten years, the total return is now approaching 138%. Therefore, investors should anticipate that the majority of their returns will come from the fund's ability to continue paying out large distributions over time.

As part of PFL's operating strategy, the fund utilizes leverage in an effort to amplify earnings. The use of leverage currently amounts to 12.03% of assets, which is equivalent to approximately $53M. I consider this to be a conservative amount of leverage, which should not significantly increase the fund's overall risk profile. However, investors still need to consider the risk that leverage can amplify losses if allocated to investments that do not perform as anticipated.

The main source of risk for PFL is the allocation to securities with poor credit ratings. For reference, securities with a credit rating below BBB, are classified as below investment grade. These are typically borrowers with less consistent cash flows or that may be reliant on debt financing to fuel operational growth. Securities with lower credit ratings are typically associated with a higher probability of being impacted by defaults.

For instance, S&P Global estimates that leveraged loan defaults can rise as high as 3.5% by March of 2026, assuming continued trade disruption related to tariffs and slowing economic growth. So for reference, the fund's breakdown by credit rating is as follows:

  • Investment Grade (AAA - BBB): 57.6%
  • Below Investment Grade (BB - D): 33.7%
  • Not Rated: 8.71%

Dividend Sustainability

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.0814 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 11.5%. If we multiply this monthly payout by twelve months, we get an estimated annual payout of $0.9768 per share. Referring back to the same annual report, PFL has demonstrated its ability to generate earnings in excess of this payout amount. However, the range of earnings has been varied, and there are plenty of years where PFL struggles to meet the necessary income level. For reference, here are the annual earnings amounts over the last few years:

  • 2019: $0.85 per share
  • 2020: ($0.55) per share
  • 2021: $2.21 per share
  • 2022: ($1.38) per share
  • 2023: $0.33 per share
  • 2024: $0.97 per share
  • 2025: $1.10 per share

As we can see, PFL has been able to generate a sufficient level of earnings in 2025. However, there are many years where PFL actively paid out more than it actually generated in income. This is why the distribution was previously lowered. If the lower interest rate environment doesn't improve the level of earnings, PFL may have to reduce dividends once more to preserve the health of the fund's NAV. This inconsistency in earnings makes it difficult to justify a long-term position for income investors. The whole reason that investors maintain exposure to these high-yield funds, is for the income that it can provide. If that income is under the threat of being reduced, it defeats the purpose of maintaining a position.

Additionally, it's worth mentioning that the majority of the distributions are not paid out using tax-efficient methods. For instance, the latest Section19(a) notice indicates that 67.80% of the payout for August was made up of net investment income. Therefore, the majority of the distribution will be classified as ordinary dividend income, which has the greatest tax burden for investors. So based on your specific tax bracket, the amount of capital invested, or desired income levels, a position in PFL would be best utilized within a tax-advantaged account to offset these potential tax consequences.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I maintain my hold rating on PFL at this time. Despite the recent interest rate cut, I remain cautious about the overall sustainability of the distributions going forward. I would like to see the fund's net investment income levels significantly improved so that the portfolio reduces reliance on positive net realized gains. The fund's inconsistent ability to generate a sufficient level of earnings to support payouts may lead to lower payouts and NAV declines. While I don't think there are any extreme major red flags that warrant a sell for existing shareholders, I will remain on the sidelines and continue to monitor how performance improves through a lower interest rate environment.
